title: Social control, conformity and resistance
paperName: Paper 1: Socialisation, identity and methods of research
summary: 9699 — formal/informal social control, conformity, deviance, and resistance to social norms.
learningObjectives: Distinguish formal and informal social control.
Explain why individuals conform to social norms.
Analyse forms of deviance and resistance.
Evaluate functionalist and Marxist views of social control.

simpleExplanation.title: Staying in line — or pushing back
simpleExplanation.summary: Societies use rewards and sanctions to encourage conformity; individuals may also resist or subvert expected behaviour.

flashcards.back: Institutional sanctions — law, police, courts, prisons.
Everyday sanctions — praise, ridicule, ostracism, stigma.
Attachment, commitment, involvement, belief — weak bonds → deviance.
Ruling-class tool protecting capitalist property relations.
Durkheim — boundary maintenance; too much/too little threatens social order.

sections.content: **Social control** (1.2) links to **1.1 socialisation**. Contrast **consensus** (functionalist) and **conflict** (Marxist/feminist) perspectives on who benefits from conformity.

sections.question: Evaluate the Marxist view that formal social control benefits the ruling class. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Marxist argument:** Law protects **private property**; Chambliss — vagrancy laws serve economic interests; selective enforcement (corporate vs street crime); ISA (school, media) + RSA (police) reproduce obedience.

**Supporting evidence:** Statistics on **class bias** in policing/sentencing; neo-Marxist Hall — moral panics over mugging.

**Criticisms:** **Functionalist** — law protects all citizens (Durkheim); **feminist** — Marxism neglects **patriarchy**; **Left realist** — working class also **victimised** by crime; not all laws serve capital (environmental, health).

**Evaluation:** Marxism **usefully highlights power** but may be **economically deterministic** — combine with **interactionism** (labelling) for fuller picture.

**Conclusion:** Partially valid — control has **class dimensions** but also **cross-class** legitimacy.
